channel.title: Posts on My Hugo Site
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on My Hugo Site

item.title: The nature of information problem
item.description:
Today in class, I learned how images are made using pixels and RGB values.
I understood how to transform images by blurring and changing them horizontally.
These activities helped me see how computers process and edit pictures.
The lesson was interesting and improved my understanding of digital images.

item.title: Class Reflection
item.description:
DIKW MODEL

In today’s class, I learned about DIKW, which means Data, Information, Knowledge, and Wisdom. Data is simple facts, and information helps us understand those facts. Knowledge teaches us how to use this information in real life. Wisdom helps us make good and correct decisions using what we learn.

item.title: **DIKW MODEL**
item.description: In the first unit of the course, I learned about the DIKW model, which stands for Data, Information, Knowledge, and Wisdom. Before taking this class, I had heard the words data and information many times, but I never clearly understood the difference between them. Through this unit, I learned that data refers to raw facts or numbers that do not have meaning by themselves. When this data is organized or given context, it becomes information.At first, the concept was new to me because I usually thought that data and information meant the same thing. However, during the class discussion, I realized that data is just raw numbers or facts without meaning. When data is organized or processed, it becomes information.With that information we learn something then it becomes knowledge . Finally, wisdom is when we use that knowledge to make good decisions.One thing I found interesting was how this model connects to everyday life. For example, a list of temperatures is data. When we organize it to see weather patterns, it becomes information. Seeing the pattern gives knowledge, and taking an umbrella because of it shows wisdom. This example helped me understand the idea more clearly. At first, I found it a little hard to remember the four levels, but drawing a pyramid and thinking about easy real-life examples made it easier. Overall, learning the DIKW model helped me see how simple data can turn into information and then knowledge. It showed me how basic facts can be useful in everyday life.
